name: Zomato-Style Restaurant Search
scenario: Build a backend search system for a food delivery platform (Zomato/Swiggy style). Restaurants are listed, customers search by cuisine, price, and rating. Each data access pattern requires a different collection — and choosing wrong makes the system slow or incorrect. This project is the Module 6 capstone: you must pick the right collection for each requirement and justify it.
description: A restaurant discovery engine using all collection types: ArrayList for master listing, HashMap for ID lookup, Map<String, List> for cuisine grouping, TreeMap for sorted price ranges, HashSet for featured restaurant IDs, and Set operations for multi-criteria filtering.

core.description: `Restaurant` class: restaurantId (String), name, cuisine (String), pricePerPerson (double), rating (double), city. Getters + toString.
`SearchEngine` with: `List<Restaurant>` allRestaurants, `Map<String, Restaurant>` byId, `Map<String, List<Restaurant>>` byCuisine, `TreeMap<Double, List<Restaurant>>` byPrice (sorted).
`searchByCuisine(String cuisine)` — returns List<Restaurant> for that cuisine, sorted by rating descending.
`findById(String id)` — O(1) lookup, returns Restaurant or null.
`inPriceRange(double min, double max)` — uses TreeMap.subMap() to get all restaurants in price range.
`topRated(int n)` — top N restaurants by rating across all cuisines. Uses Collections.sort on a copy.
main() demonstrates all five operations with 8+ restaurants across 3 cuisines.

bonus.description: Add `Set<String>` featuredIds. `feature(String id)` marks as featured. `getFeatured()` returns List<Restaurant> for featured IDs.
Add `searchMultiCuisine(Set<String> cuisines)` — returns restaurants matching ANY of the given cuisines (union of cuisine results), deduplicated.
Add `cityStats(String city)` — Map<String, Object> with: count (int), avgRating (double), cuisines (Set<String>).

data_to_use:
Restaurants:
- R001, Spice Garden, Indian, ₹350/person, 4.3, Hyderabad
- R002, Noodle House, Chinese, ₹280/person, 4.1, Hyderabad
- R003, Pizza Place, Italian, ₹500/person, 4.5, Mumbai
- R004, Curry Leaf, Indian, ₹400/person, 4.7, Mumbai
- R005, Wok & Roll, Chinese, ₹320/person, 4.2, Hyderabad
- R006, Biryani Bros, Indian, ₹300/person, 4.4, Hyderabad
- R007, Pasta Corner, Italian, ₹550/person, 4.0, Mumbai
- R008, Dim Sum Delight, Chinese, ₹450/person, 4.6, Mumbai
example_output:
=== RESTAURANT SEARCH ENGINE ===

--- Indian Restaurants (by rating) ---
Curry Leaf: 4.7 ⭐ ₹400
Biryani Bros: 4.4 ⭐ ₹300
Spice Garden: 4.3 ⭐ ₹350

--- Find by ID ---
R005: Wok & Roll (Chinese)

--- Price ₹300-₹400 ---
Noodle House (₹280)... wait, 300-400 range:
Wok & Roll, Biryani Bros, Spice Garden, Curry Leaf...

--- Top 3 Rated ---
1. Curry Leaf (4.7)
2. Dim Sum Delight (4.6)
3. Pizza Place (4.5)

criteria: Comments explain why each collection chosen (10 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 hints. The key challenge is `inPriceRange` with TreeMap.subMap(min, true, max, true). Also, searchByCuisine needs to sort by rating descending — use a Comparator.
submission_instructions: Save as RestaurantSearch.java. Add a comment above each data structure field explaining WHY that collection type was chosen. Push to GitHub — this demonstrates Module 6 mastery in a recognizable real-world context.
tips_for_success: Comment every data structure: // HashMap: O(1) lookup by restaurant ID.
addRestaurant must add to ALL four structures — easy to forget one.
inPriceRange: byPrice.subMap(min, true, max, true).values() gives you the matching price bands. Loop through each band's list.
searchByCuisine: get the list, make a copy, sort by rating descending (Double.compare(b.rating, a.rating)).
topRated: copy allRestaurants, sort descending, return first n.
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: This project forces you to think about EACH data access pattern separately and choose the right tool. Real Zomato/Swiggy backends make these exact choices. The TreeMap for price-range queries is particularly elegant — `subMap()` is the built-in range slice that makes range queries efficient without writing any loop logic. After this project, collection choice feels natural.
